speaker: interviewer
text: November 26, 1996. Interview with Sonia Bielski, born, Boldo. My name is Lillian Gewirtzman, Brooklyn, New York, USA. The language is English. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] November 26, 1996, interview with Sonia Bielski, born, Boldo. My name is Lillian Gewirtzman, G-E-W-I-R-T-Z-M-A-N, Brooklyn, New York, USA. The language is English. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Good afternoon, Mrs. Bielski. Would you please tell us your full name, and spell it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sonia Bielski, B-I-E-L-S-K-I.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Boldo, B-O-L-D-O.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you tell us the name of the town and country where you were born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Novogrudok, Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your birth date?

speaker: interviewer
text: How old does that make you now?

speaker: interviewee
text: I forgot already. It makes me 74, 75, almost.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Meshulam Boldo, Meshulam.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was he born?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was born in Lubcz. That's a small village. That's not a city, a village, near Novogrudok, not far from Novogrudok.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is the largest big town that Novogrudok borders on?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lida Baranowicze, and not far away from Vilna.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your mother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's name was Pesia [? Noshemovski. ?] No-she-movski.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was she born?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was born in a village, not far from Novogrudok, in Baranovichi.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any siblings?

speaker: interviewee
text: Me?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had four brothers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you give us their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. My oldest brother was Mordechai. Second was Itzchak. The third I was. And then was Zalman, and Yankele.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have an extended family? Grandparents, aunts?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. My grandparents died in Israel about 10 years ago. They were together with us in the war. And after the war, they wanted to go to Israel. So my husband bought an apartment in Israel, and they went to Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you had grandparents when you were a child?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. In the war. Not grandparents, no. My mother and father. I didn't have grandparents already. My grandparents, they die in Poland, not from the Germans, by themselves, from old age.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, sure, I remember. My mother's father was Rabbi Hirsh. When he died, he was 105 years old, a beautiful, beautiful old man. And the people around him, they were coming, he should bless them always. And my grandma was probably about 98 years. She lived after he died. She never knew that he died. But we had a woman and a man. They were taking care on them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember spending any time with your grandparents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. They lived not far from us, about two streets from us. My mother was the youngest child there. So she was always there. When we went to school, something, she was coming, and see if everything is taken care. And we always came Sunday or Saturday, or when there was a holiday. We saw always grandpa in the synagogue. He had a synagogue not far from the house where he lived.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his function in the synagogue?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, he was a rabbi. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any other close relatives?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. My father had a father, Shmuel. But he died very young. I remember when he died. He was about 50 or 60 years old. I remember the night when he died. And his mother was not this mother, but was the second mother. But she was so good to him, that we called her grandma.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: I couldn't-- her name was-- wait a second. I put in on the paper. Now I don't remember already. Whatever. But she was wonderful. She was so good when my grandpa died. We was holding her like she was really a grandma.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was she living with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. We had a big house. And this was three apartments. This was apartment special for them, but in the same house.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you like to describe your house for me?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we had a big house on [? Tertmay ?], in Novogrudok. We had about six rooms, and a big kitchen, and a big dining room. I had-- my brothers, they were together in two rooms. I had my room myself. My mother had a big room, father and mother's. And all the parties, they were made in my mother's room. This was like a ballroom. I had too a very beautiful room.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your room.

speaker: interviewee
text: My room was separate from my brothers, a little further. And the walls were covered with Persian rugs. This was two big Persian rugs on the walls and one on the floor. And I have a big couch, a big bed. This was like a couch, not a bed. And I had my table, because I work in there. When the war began, I have here friends of mine. She was not my friend, she was friends of my brothers, Zalman, Sula Wolozynska. She-- her house was demolished. She lived with the father and mother. They had-- her father was a dentist, and the mother was a dentist. And she had another sister. One sister was Sula. And the other was Rita.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she lived with you, the friend?

speaker: interviewee
text: Wait a second. The house was demolished. They couldn't find a place. So my mother said to her mother, you know what? Go ask Sonia if she is ready to give to you her room. If Sonia will give you her room, I will gladly accept you to my house. OK? And her father and mother came to us. And we were sitting quietly. Because this was already, the German was around and fighting, and throwing the bombs from all over. So we're sitting quietly. And they opened the door, and they come in.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they said, Sonia, your mother said, if you can give me a room. I said, why should I give you my room? She said, because I don't have where to go to sleep. I said, gladly, I will give you my room. And they were six weeks in my room, four of them. And she, when you will have a chance, she will describe you my room.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was Persian rugs on the walls and a big Persian rug on the floor. And my couch was for two people. So my mother and father put them another bed, two bed. And they lived in my room for six weeks. And they ate with us. And they lived with us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you stay?

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you stay at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I stayed with my mother in her room, with my mother and father.

speaker: interviewer
text: What room did you spend most of the time in when you were at home? Where did the family gather?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had a dining room, a big dining room. When we came from school, about two o'clock, not later, we had supper. Supper was not at night like here. But supper was between one and two o'clock. We had a woman, she was working 14 or 15 years, an old [INAUDIBLE]. A woman from the village. And she was sleeping by us, and working. And she was cooking. And we had another cousin of my mother's, what she didn't have nobody in the whole world. And my father went, before the war, he went and he brought her in to our house. And she stayed with us, and lived with us, till the end of her life.

speaker: interviewer
text: So and I slept with my mother and father in the room. We had another two rooms, small ones. This was for the servant. And one was for Eshke, my mother's cousin. You remember the servant's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: I forgot. Aneczka! Aneczka. You see, when I talking, you remember. And I remember like that, that about two years, three years before the war broke up, my mother send you to United States. Because she had a son in the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who did?

speaker: interviewee
text: Aneczka. The woman, who she was working for us, from the village, she had a son. And her husband went to United States. And he took away her son from her. But after a couple of years, he began to write to us that he would like the mother should come to United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my mother took her to Warsaw. She made a papers for her. And she send her off to United States. What were the religious practices of the home?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was very religious, orthodox. My father, I cannot say that he was very orthodox, but he loved her very much. And he did everything what my mother wanted. So he was too. He went to the synagogue. He was a rabbi in the synagogue. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] he observed all the Jewish things, what he had to.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you like to tell us about a holiday in your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: The holiday in the house was something to remember. We never ate the suppers for ourselves. We had always people [NON-ENGLISH]. You know what [NON-ENGLISH]? This is people what they didn't have the families. They were studying Torah, in the city. And they didn't have where to eat. My mother and father, they were holding 19 boys. They were eating in time from the week, 19 boys. They were changing. They were eating by us. I will tell you another story. My husband had a brother, a rabbi. And he too lived in Novogrudok. They were not too rich. It was a big family. And he was eating by us days, the rabbi.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you mean by eating days?

speaker: interviewee
text: His day was Monday, or Tuesday, right? So this was divided. The whole week was divided between the 19 boys, yes? And my mother took in the Bielski [INAUDIBLE]. He was a rabbi. And he was Zusia's brother. They were not religious. But this one was very religious.

speaker: interviewer
text: Whose brother was he?

speaker: interviewee
text: Zusia. Zusia [INAUDIBLE]. And he was eating by us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Zusia was whom?

speaker: interviewee
text: Zusia was my husband.

speaker: interviewer
text: Later?

speaker: interviewee
text: And Tuvia. Yeah. And sure, later. And Tuvia's, and Asael's and [? Itzchak's. ?] And he was starting in Novogrudok. Probably didn't have enough for the food. So he was asking where can he go to eat, because he was very kosher.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they said, you know what? You ask the Boldos. There is kosher food. So we took him every week. One day of the week he was eating by us. So how did a dinner like that look?

speaker: interviewee
text: The dinner was-- you see, my father-- we had two [NON-ENGLISH]. This is farms. One farm was ours. And the other farm was [NON-ENGLISH]. From the Polack.

speaker: interviewer
text: And my father was making cheeses, like from Holland, you have the round cheeses. And they were coming from Vilna, from Warsaw with the trucks to take away the cheeses. Right? Now, he was buying meat for the Polish army. So we had everything. We had meat, there were cheeses, and butter, and everything. There was not a thing that we didn't have it. Was that your father's business?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my father's business was, right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Making cheese.

speaker: interviewee
text: Cheese, and meat. And we had the chickens and [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: And selling those provisions to whom?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. We didn't sell it. No. The meat was send off to the Polish army. The cheeses were sent off to the big stores in Vilna and Warsaw. But there is enough was for us too. You understand? We had enough, everything. We had two kibbutzim for [NON-ENGLISH] in Novogrudok. And my father and mother, they gave to them two houses, from [NON-ENGLISH]. And they were sending them bread, and butter, and cheeses. My whole family brothers, they were [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they by the two houses? You said your father gave them two houses?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We had about three houses. So when the people moved out from there, my brother, [INAUDIBLE], said, Papi, we need the house for the kibbutz. They're coming from all over the [NON-ENGLISH]. We need a house.

speaker: interviewer
text: He said, OK. You know what? Take the house and make a kibbutz. And that was a kibbutz.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was across our house. And when come Monday, or Thursday when [NON-ENGLISH], you know, when everybody was coming to the market, they were sending him cheeses, and butter, and whatever they needed, and bread, everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your family do for recreation?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, my mother loved movies, and loved reading. She was reading a lot. She was a [NON-ENGLISH]. She was working very hard. She wanted the children to know everything. We had tutors in the house, Hebrew tutors.

speaker: interviewer
text: I remember that a rabbi was coming and was studying with the boys [NON-ENGLISH]. You know what it is? [NON-ENGLISH]. I remember now. Because they were [NON-ENGLISH]. They were not very religious. So my mother said, OK. You're not religious, but I want you to know about the religion because I'm a Jew and you are a Jew too. So they gave it to them everything in a house. You understand? So they knew everything. They're studying everything. That's it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And full with friends. Full with friends. The house was full with friends always. Saturday night, were about 10, 15, people eating by us. Saturday night was like this written in the calendar. They were eating potatoes, herring, cream. Everything [NON-ENGLISH], not meat.

speaker: interviewer
text: Dairy.

speaker: interviewee
text: Dairy. And for sitting about 15, 12 people. And they were talking about the politics, and about Israel, and everything. This was it. I was not always there, the truth because I didn't believe in this. I went to a Polish [NON-ENGLISH], a Polish school, where only 2%, 3% Jewish girls and boys went. So I was far away from them. I never belonged to [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Far away where?

speaker: interviewee
text: I far away from this [NON-ENGLISH]. You see, my brothers were in the [NON-ENGLISH]. They were working there. And they took in many boys and girls, but they didn't have it how to study.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there, in the [NON-ENGLISH], they gave it to him. This for study with them. They were learning about Israel. They're learning about [NON-ENGLISH] Israel, and everything. What language was spoken at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yiddish. But my brothers were very fluently in Hebrew. That's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of school did you attend?

speaker: interviewee
text: Me? I was attending high school, from the [NON-ENGLISH]. That means from the-- [NON-ENGLISH] mean from the-- from the Polish state. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was attending there the high school. And after the high school, I attended [NON-ENGLISH], like two years college here. This was French, history, Polish, and Latin. And after that, I did the two years. My mother and father wanted me to go to Sorbonne, for dentistry. Because I had a very big family in Paris, and very rich. And they said, let's send Sonia here. And we'll give her the qualification what she wanted. And in '37, 1937, my mother took me to Paris to see the family, if I like it, to go there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you like it?

speaker: interviewee
text: For a while I liked it, but I was very miserable. I didn't know how to talk French. I was miserable. I studied in school, but this was not enough. So when I came after that, my mommy took me, a madame. A madame, a teacher, a French, where she was coming two hours a day. And she was conversation with me in French. When the war began, I was better speaking French than I'm speaking now English. But with the time, even you're not speaking a language, you forgetting. I'm reading. And I'm writing.

speaker: interviewer
text: What goals did you have for yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: What ambitions did you have for yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had big ambitions. First of all, I wanted to go from Novogrudok. A time from Noel, that's [NON-ENGLISH]. That's New Year, the Polish, yes? My father was taking me always to Vilna or to Warsaw. They were buying me everything what I were thinking about it, a jewelry, shoes. They were not buying me in the stores, but they were making orders for me. I was only 15, 16. And they were making already orders [NON-ENGLISH], Vilna. This was the biggest stores for the making the shoes. They made shoes for me there. They made a fur coat for me. I have a jacket for me, from mink. I wanted to go to see the world. When I came in '37, we were there three weeks in Paris. I saw the world there. I didn't see that in Novogrudok. I saw Novogrudok as a village then. Till I went out from Novogrudok, I was thinking that's a big city.

speaker: interviewer
text: But when I came, I said to my mother, you know what? This is not-- it's a village. She said, my [INAUDIBLE]. Because you saw Paris. What kind of a neighborhood did you live in Novogrudok? Who were your neighbors?

speaker: interviewee
text: A doctor, and a church. We have about two Jewish families. And the rest of them, they were Polish. And they were very good. We were very friendly with them. My father was working, and they give it to them everything, everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Somebody was knocking at the door, and my father and mother ask them, what do you need? We need something, whatever. My father and mother went there right away. They give it to them.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the time before the war, there were Polish people what they were knocking at the door, and the windows, all over. To us, they didn't knock. They were telling to my father, please, close the windows and everything. And don't go out in this and this hours outside.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your immediate neighbors?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about the general population? What kind of a town was Novogrudok? Was it an industrial town? Was it a farming town?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Novogrudok was a town, mostly they were a Jewish town. Mostly they were Jewish people. Later, in the time when they built the big things, after Mickiewicz. This is a romantic, a Polish romantic. I think everybody know in the literature who is Mickiewicz was. Adam Mickiewicz.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was a big writer, a Polish. So when they began to build in his name many things. They got [NON-ENGLISH]. What is [NON-ENGLISH]? All Pole. Poland was divided now [INAUDIBLE]. The biggest cities was [INAUDIBLE]. The smaller [INAUDIBLE] was not the [INAUDIBLE]. Because Mickiewicz was born in Novogrudok, they gave it to Novogrudok the [INAUDIBLE]. So then the Polish people began to come in to Novogrudok. They begin big offices. How big a population did Novogrudok have?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think about 15,000, 18,000 people, no more. But this was enough.

speaker: interviewer
text: How big was the Jewish population of Novogrudok?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think about 8,000.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the occupation of most of the Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: You see, our city had many Jews, and they were very rich. I had a friend, Zaria, which she's now in Israel. They had [NON-ENGLISH]. You know what is [NON-ENGLISH]? From the tree they were making for building the [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Lumber yard.

speaker: interviewee
text: Lumber yard, yes. Lumber yard. And they were very rich. They were about four brothers. And they had children. And they were very, very, rich.

speaker: interviewer
text: There was another family, [? Broido. ?] They were too very, very rich. They were making from the corn, they were making flours. And they were giving a lot to the Polish [NON-ENGLISH], I mean to the Polish-- for the-- like you see in our organization, yes? So for the Polish people. So they didn't fight the Jew, the big ones. But we had many Jewish people what they were very, very poor. So we had for them, the old people, we had places for where they took the old people there. And everybody was giving them bread, and everything. They called this like old age, old ages.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the rich one, they were holding onto them. They giving them everything. But mostly, we cannot talk about this 5%, or the 3% they were very rich. Rest of them, they were very, very poor. I don't know how they lived. They didn't die from hunger. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were their occupations? What did they do?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think they were working on shuls. They were working on dresses. And many, many didn't work at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about the cultural life? Were there theater groups? Were there movies?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes, was movies. Three movies we had in Novogrudok. Three movies. We had the theater who once in a month, they were coming.

speaker: interviewer
text: Jewish theater?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not Jewish. This come from Warsaw. It was coming Polish.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about the Jewish cultural life?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was some life. But I didn't belong there. Because this was in Jewish language.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a Jewish library?

speaker: interviewee
text: Probably, yes. But I was not into the library. I went to the Polish library. Because in our school, where there were the 3% Jewish people, they were asking us not to talk Yiddish, because of the language.

speaker: interviewer
text: We were talking the language. This was like a [INAUDIBLE]. It's not a clean Polish. And they were making that you have to improve your language if you want to go to school. You understand? So there were reading enough Polish books. We had libraries in the gymnasium. And we had for this week, we had to finish two books. What would you say the relationship, generally, of the Jewish population was with the non-Jewish population?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not so good. Not so good.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you like to tell me about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I will tell you. I know the non-Jewish, they hate us. The Polish people, they hate us, the Jewish. They were saying, heh, [NON-ENGLISH]. You came for [NON-ENGLISH]. You know what is [NON-ENGLISH]? You came to beg, to beg something. What do you want to beg? You want money? Go to Israel. You don't have to beg us for something.

speaker: interviewer
text: There was [NON-ENGLISH]. You know what is a [NON-ENGLISH]? [NON-ENGLISH] is now you have Blue Cross and Blue Shield. In Poland you were not paying for that. You had. So when they came, and this was a couple of Jew waiting, the Polish was the first. Always the first. The Jew was always the last one. We have to break now.
